elements.sent-2411-001: Spiking neuronal networks (SNNs) are widely used in computational neuroscience, from biologically realistic modeling of local cortical networks to phenomenological modeling of the whole brain.
elements.sent-2411-002: Despite their prevalence, a systematic mathematical theory for finite-sized SNNs remains elusive, even for idealized homogeneous networks.
elements.sent-2411-003: The primary challenges are twofold: 1) the rich, parameter-sensitive SNN dynamics, and 2) the singularity and irreversibility of spikes.
elements.sent-2411-004: These challenges pose significant difficulties when relating SNNs to systems of differential equations, leading previous studies to impose additional assumptions or to focus on individual dynamic regimes.
elements.sent-2411-005: In this study, we introduce a Markov approximation of homogeneous SNN dynamics to minimize information loss when translating SNNs into ordinary differential equations.
elements.sent-2411-006: Our only assumption for the Markov approximation is the fast self-decorrelation of synaptic conductances.
elements.sent-2411-007: The system of ordinary differential equations derived from the Markov model effectively captures high-frequency partial synchrony and the metastability of finite-neuron networks produced by interacting excitatory and inhibitory populations.
elements.sent-2411-008: Besides accurately predicting dynamical statistics, such as firing rates, our theory also quantitatively captures the geometry of attractors and bifurcation structures of SNNs.
elements.sent-2411-009: Thus, our work provides a comprehensive mathematical framework that can systematically map parameters of single-neuron physiology, network coupling, and external stimuli to homogeneous SNN dynamics.
elements.sent-2411-010: With minimal assumptions, a Markov framework counting the number of neurons in each state captures population dynamics of finite-sized, homogeneous spiking neural networks.
elements.sent-2411-011: The expectation of the random path is derived as a set of ordinary differential equations (ODE) for discretized neuronal states.
elements.sent-2411-012: Our ODE system well-approximates the dynamical statistics and attractors of the corresponding spiking neuronal networks, including near bifurcation points.
elements.sent-2411-013: Spiking neuronal networks (SNNs) are widely used in computational neuroscience to model and simulate neuronal networks ranging from local patches in cortex or the hippocampus to larger-scale neural pathways and processes involved in sensory integration and decision making \cite{wang2002probabilistic,izhikevich2008large,eliasmith2012large,potjans2014cell,zenke2015diverse,markram2015reconstruction,bezaire2016interneuronal,chariker2016orientation,ramirez2018dissecting,schmidt2018multi,billeh2020systematic,siegle2021survey,zenke2021remarkable,chariker2022computational .}
elements.sent-2411-014: In contrast to rate-based network models, where the population firing rates is the carrier of information, SNN captures the fine-grained dynamics where neurons communicate with discrete electrical impulses, i.e., action potentials or spikes.
elements.sent-2411-015: In the brain, spikes may carry information not only through mean frequencies but also via precise timing \cite{bialek1989reading,mainen1995reliability,decharms1996primary,BialekWarlandSteveninck1997,singer1999neuronal,tiesinga2008regulation .}
elements.sent-2411-016: For instance, temporal precision is critical in learning mechanisms such as spike-timing dependent plasticity (STDP), where the relative timing of spikes between neurons instructs the strengthening or weakening of their synaptic connections \cite{bi1998synaptic,song2000competitive,dan2004spike,brzosko2019neuromodulation,payeur2021burst .}
elements.sent-2411-017: Furthermore, biological neurons are sensitive to spike timing when they receive inputs \cite{bialek1989reading,KonigEtAl1996,singer1999neuronal,brette2012computing .}
elements.sent-2411-018: This sensitivity can induce partial and transient synchrony within neural circuits, leading to temporally coordinated activities \cite{singer1999neuronal, wang2010neurophysiological .}
elements.sent-2411-019: The precision of neuronal spikes is essential for understanding how neural circuits encode, transmit, and process information \cite{johansson2004first,fries2015rhythms,panzeri2017cracking,lundqvist2016gamma .}
elements.sent-2411-020: Neural oscillations arising from synchronized neuronal activity play a pivotal role in coordinating communication between different brain regions and facilitating cognitive processes \cite{buzsaki2004neuronal,siegle2021survey .}
elements.sent-2411-021: Similarly, previous studies on SNNs also show that small variability in spike timing can lead to vastly different overall network dynamics, including significant changes in synchrony and the emergence or suppression of neural oscillations \cite{izhikevich2004model,schmidt2018multi .}
elements.sent-2411-022: Thus, a unified mathematical theory for SNNs is highly sought after.
elements.sent-2411-023: Ideally, such a theory would provide a mapping that directly relates external inputs, network architecture, and single-neuronal physiology to the dynamics and function of the SNN, allowing for accurate predictions of network synchrony and neural oscillatory behavior.
elements.sent-2411-024: However, developing a comprehensive mathematical theory faces significant challenges.
elements.sent-2411-025: First, SNNs can exhibit a wide range of dynamical behaviors, from stable, fixed points to oscillatory, metastable, and chaotic dynamics \cite{van1996chaos,izhikevich2004model,gerstner2014neuronal,nobukawa2017chaotic .}
elements.sent-2411-026: Previous studies show that SNNs are highly sensitive to parameter choices \cite{brunel2000dynamics,izhikevich2008large,mejias2010irregular,landau2016impact,xiao2021data .}
elements.sent-2411-027: For example, Xiao et al. (2021) report that a mere 1\% change in synaptic coupling weights can disrupt biologically realistic firing rates.
elements.sent-2411-028: Second, the singularity and irreversibility of spikes lead to great difficulty for classical analytical tools based on differential equations \cite{ostojic2011spiking,schwalger2017towards,MontbrioEtAl2015,buice2013dynamic .}
elements.sent-2411-029: In SNNs, spikes are often modeled as singular signals between neurons.
elements.sent-2411-030: The physiological process of a neuron generating a spike—surpassing a threshold and then resetting—is irreversible, unlike mutual interactions in Newtonian systems \cite{hodgkin1952quantitative,izhikevich2004model .}
elements.sent-2411-031: This irreversibility and singularity, combined with the asymmetry of spike projections, means that the precise timing of even a single spike may significantly influence the overall SNN dynamics \cite{DiesmannEtAl1999,zillmer2006desynchronization,london2010sensitivity .}
elements.sent-2411-032: Additionally, real brain networks are neither too small nor infinitely large.
elements.sent-2411-033: This makes SNNs high-dimensional systems, but assuming $N \to \infty$ neglects fluctuations and variations inherent in local neural circuits with finite numbers of neurons.
elements.sent-2411-034: This is especially the case in fundamental functional units containing only $O(10)-O(10^3)$ neurons, such as minicolumns and hypercolumns \cite{mountcastle1997columnar,rockel1980basic,buxhoeveden2002minicolumn,feldmeyer2013barrel .}
elements.sent-2411-035: Many approaches have been proposed to address these challenges.
elements.sent-2411-036: These include neural field models, Fokker-Planck equations, kinetic theories, refractory density methods, Kuramoto-type methods, and so on \cite{wilson1972excitatory,wilson1973mathematical,cai2006kinetic,vinci2023self,schwalger2017towards,MontbrioEtAl2015,buice2013beyond .}
elements.sent-2411-037: Each of these methods typically requires additional assumptions that facilitate the derivation of differential equations for the population dynamics.
elements.sent-2411-038: For example, neural field equations assume predefined forms of interactions between average rates of spiking events, and therefore, does not apply to spiking dynamics \cite{buice2013dynamic,ostojic2011spiking ; Fokker-Planck and kinetic theory models assume infinite neuron numbers, weak coupling, and low degrees of transient synchrony; moreover, refractory density methods assume identical inputs to all neurons.}
elements.sent-2411-039: These assumptions come with a trade-off: the further they deviate from biological realism, the more the resulting theory is confined to specific dynamic regimes, making the model less generally applicable to the complex dynamics observed in real brain networks.
elements.sent-2411-040: In this study, we propose a Markovian framework that reduces SNNs comprising of multiple homogeneous populations to a set of differential equations.
elements.sent-2411-041: We minimize the information loss of SNN dynamics by only assuming the fast decorrelation of synaptic conductances from their own history.
elements.sent-2411-042: This assumption, necessary for coarse-graining, makes neurons in the same state interchangeable.
elements.sent-2411-043: It is also a natural assumption for neuronal networks with homogeneous architecture.
elements.sent-2411-044: To approximate SNN dynamics, our theory employs a Markov model with discrete states, and the irreversible spike-generating process and singularity of spiking impact become merely state transitions within the Markov model.
elements.sent-2411-045: Its Markovian nature also provides unique analytical benefits, such as invariant probability measures, transitions between multiple attractors, further model reductions, and more (see Discussion).
elements.sent-2411-046: We then derive a set of ordinary differential equations (termed discrete-state ODEs or dsODEs) from the coarse-grained Markov model by counting the number of neurons in each state.
elements.sent-2411-047: It provides both quantitative predictions for SNN dynamics and convenience for mathematical analysis.
elements.sent-2411-048: The whole approach is demonstrated on leaky, integrate-and-fire neuronal networks that are widely used in computational neuroscience and brain modeling, and can also be extended to other types of SNNs with more biological details.
elements.sent-2411-049: Compared to previous mathematical theories of SNNs, our framework directly addresses challenges of biologically realistic modeling setups, such as partially synchronous dynamics, fluctuation-driven dynamics, variations from a finite number of neurons, and strong recurrent couplings.
elements.sent-2411-050: These advantages are gained by discretizing and fixing neuronal states, so one can directly count the neuronal flux between states for arbitrarily small timestep $\delta t$.
elements.sent-2411-051: In other words, we sacrifice the ``spatial accuracy" of neuronal state to gain the flexibility to account for transient synchrony and noise, represented by the neuronal flux between states (see more in Sect.~\ref{Sect5-Comparison ).}
elements.sent-2411-052: This paper is structured as follows.
elements.sent-2411-053: Section \ref{Sect2-Markov presents the Markov approximation of SNNs on leaky integrate-and-fire (LIF) networks and introduces its coarse-grained version.}
elements.sent-2411-054: In Section \ref{Sect3-SDE , we derive the dsODEs from the coarse-grained Markov model.}
elements.sent-2411-055: Readers not interested in mathematical details can directly proceed to Section \ref{Sect4-Simulation , where we systematically compare dsODEs with LIF network simulations across parameter space.}
elements.sent-2411-056: Section \ref{Sect5-Comparison presents the comparison between dsODE and previous population methods, including Wilson-Cowan neural field equations, Fokker-Planck equations, and refractory density methods.}
elements.sent-2411-057: Section \ref{Sect6-Diss concludes our results and discusses future directions.}
elements.sent-2411-058: We present a Markovian framework on SNNs consisting of leaky integrate-and-fire (LIF) neurons.
elements.sent-2411-059: Potential extensions to other types of spiking neurons are discussed in Sect \ref{Sect6-Diss .}
elements.sent-2411-060: In this section, we first recall the general formulation of an LIF network, before introducing our Markov framework.
elements.sent-2411-061: Then, for an SNN consisting of homogeneous neural populations, we derive a coarse-grained (CG) version of the Markov model focusing on the number of neurons in each state.
elements.sent-2411-062: The only assumption for the CG model is the fast self-decorrelation of recurrent synaptic drives to each neuron, leading to the interchangeability of neurons within each state.
elements.sent-2411-063: The formulation of LIF networks is standard and can be found in \cite{gerstner2014neuronal .}
elements.sent-2411-064: Throughout this paper, we focus on LIF networks consisting of homogeneous neuronal populations (that is, within each sub-population, all neurons have identical physiological properties).

elements.subsection-2411-002: A Markovian approximation of LIF networks
elements.sent-2411-080: This subsection presents our Markov framework as a surrogate of LIF networks described in Sect.~\ref{Sect2.1-LIF .}
elements.sent-2411-081: The primary idea is to discretize the voltage of each neuron ($V_i$) into a finite state space.
elements.sent-2411-082: The drives $(I_i^{\mathrm{ext ,g_i^E, g_i^I)$ are discretized accordingly, and their consequence on voltages induce transitions between states.}}

elements.sent-2411-149: The goal of Assumption 1 is to coarse-grain the Markov model by considering only the distribution of neuron numbers in different states of potentials \(V\).

elements.section-2411-004: Deriving discrete-state ODEs from coarse-grained Markov models
elements.sent-2411-181: In this section, we first derive a set of stochastic differential equations (SDE) for all variables in Eq.~\ref{Sect2.3-Eqn7-CGfunction with Gaussian approximations of the recurrent pending kick pools ($H$s) and the flux of neurons between different neuron states.}
elements.sent-2411-182: The Gaussian approximation requires large numbers of neurons in each state.
elements.sent-2411-183: To account for the finite-N effect, we then reduce this system to a lower-dimensional ODE system by combining consecutive voltage bins.
elements.sent-2411-184: This reduced ODE system is thus termed “discrete-state ordinary differential equations” (dsODEs).
elements.sent-2411-185: We first need to ``continuize" the discrete $n$s and $H$s to obtain a differential system for temporal dynamics.
elements.sent-2411-186: Here, the SDE for neuron numbers can be regarded as the master equations for $n^Q_m$ when $N^{E,I}\to\infty$.
elements.sent-2411-187: Two ODEs trace the expectations and variances of each $H$, which in turn drive the SDEs for neuron numbers in different states.

elements.sent-2411-188: We first derive differential equations of $H^{QE}$.

elements.figure-2411-002: \begin{center} \includegraphics*[bb=0in 3in 11in 15.2in,width=0.75\textwidth]{{figures/1-the-core-idea-of-dsODE}.jpg} \caption{Schematic diagrams for model reductions and spiking network studied in this paper. A. A chain of model reductions. From left to right: leaky-integrate-fire (LIF) network with E \& I populations, where neuron states ($V$) are continuous (indicated by warm \& cold color codes); the Markovian spiking network, where neuron states ($v$) are discretized; the coarse-grained Markov network, where neurons in the same state are treated as interchangeable; and finally, the dsODE system, where the number of neurons in each state is treated as continuous. B. States of the LIF network and the corresponding dsODE system at a given time point. Left: the upper raster plot displays spiking events across all neurons within a time window. elements.section-2411-005: dsODEs approximate simulations of spiking networks
elements.sent-2411-259: We perform a systematic comparison between the dynamics of the LIF network and the dsODE system.
elements.sent-2411-260: This section demonstrates that, when coupled with the same parameter choices, dsODEs accurately capture the initial transient dynamics, long-term behaviors, and finite-N fluctuations of the LIF network dynamics.
elements.sent-2411-261: Additionally, dsODEs successfully predict the bifurcation points in the LIF network dynamics across all parameter investigations, even during transitions between temporal homogeneity, synchrony, and oscillations across multiple frequency bands.
elements.sent-2411-262: Furthermore, dsODEs quantitatively predict the transition waiting times when the LIF network dynamics exhibit metastability.
elements.sent-2411-263: Throughout the remainder of the paper, we fix $\mathbf{M = 20$ states.}
elements.sent-2411-264: While this section primarily focuses on the dsODE system, we also test the dsSDE scheme in a few examples to recover critical noise-induced dynamical features of spiking networks.
elements.sent-2411-265: The dsSDE scheme is tested more systematically when we perform bifurcation analysis in Sect.~\ref{Sect4.5-Bifurcation , and when we investigate finite-N fluctuations in Sect.~\ref{Sect4.6-FiniteN .}}

elements.sent-2411-328: An ultimate test for the dsODE system is to perform a bifurcation analysis for the corresponding spiking neural networks (SNNs).
elements.sent-2411-329: Bifurcation analysis is a powerful tool in nonlinear dynamical systems, revealing how qualitative changes in a system’s behavior arise from small variations in parameters.
elements.sent-2411-330: However, this analysis is particularly challenging for dsODEs to detect the drastic changes in SNN dynamics that occur around bifurcation points, including multi-stability, oscillatory behaviors, or even chaotic dynamics, as shown by the examples in Sect.~\ref{Sect4.3-1E1I and below.}
elements.sent-2411-331: Therefore, capturing such delicate phenomena requires a mathematical theory that accurately reflects the underlying system dynamics, rather than one merely relying on data fitting.
elements.sent-2411-332: We perform bifurcation analysis on SNNs composed of a pair of E/I populations, focusing on a dynamical feature called the “beat index.” During oscillatory dynamics in SNNs, the size and duration of spiking clusters may vary over time.
elements.sent-2411-333: The beat index refers to the number of spiking clusters between the recurrence of two clusters with similar sizes and durations (see more details of the beat index in \cite{wu2022multi ).}
elements.sent-2411-334: For example, in Fig.~\ref{Fig2_LIF_dynamics C, the beat index is 1 (or “1-beat”) for $S^{EI = 2.45$, but increases to 2-beat for $S^{EI = 2.95$, where strong and weak clusters alternate.}}}
elements.sent-2411-336: Accurately predicting the beat index requires capturing SNN dynamics across different time scales—both within and between spiking clusters—making the corresponding bifurcation analysis a true test of the robustness and accuracy of dsODEs.
elements.sent-2411-337: The visualization of beat indices in bifurcation maps has been detailed in our previous study \cite{wu2022multi .}
elements.sent-2411-338: Briefly, the size and duration of each spiking cluster are determined by the state of the SNN at the onset of the cluster, denoted by $t_i$.
elements.sent-2411-339: A key indicator of the SNN state is $\bar{m (t_i) = \bar{V _E(t_i) - \bar{V _I(t_i)$, which represents the difference between the average membrane potentials of the E and I populations at time $t_i$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-340: Intuitively, a large $\bar{m}$ implies an advantage of the E population in the E-I competition, leading to a larger spiking cluster, and vice versa.
elements.sent-2411-341: For visualization purpose, we track $\Delta \bar{m (t_i) = \bar{m (t_{i+1 ) - \bar{m (t_i)$, the difference between consecutive $\bar{m}$ values.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-342: The beat index is then represented by the number of peaks in the distribution of $\Delta \bar{m}$.
elements.sent-2411-343: For example, in 1-beat dynamics, $\Delta \bar{m \approx 0$, while 2-beat dynamics alternate between two distinct $\bar{m}$ values, resulting in $\Delta \bar{m = \pm |\bar{m (t_{i+1 ) - \bar{m (t_i)|$, and so on.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-344: Figure~\ref{Fig7_bifurcations presents bifurcation maps for eight key parameters of the spiking network, encompassing synaptic coupling weights, projection probability, and physiological timescales.}
elements.sent-2411-345: Each map shows the density of spiking clusters, characterized by the difference in initial population voltage $\Delta \bar{m}$ from one cluster to the next.
elements.sent-2411-346: The colormaps, normalized to the standard Gamma frequency (40 Hz), indicate the frequency of spiking clusters.
elements.sent-2411-347: That is, the more intense red areas represent higher frequencies, and vice versa.
elements.sent-2411-348: Notably, regions with no density signify convergence of dsODEs to point attractors, implying the absence of spiking clusters in the temporal dynamics (e.g., for low $S^{II}$ and high variance in external inputs).
elements.sent-2411-349: In each bifurcation map, one parameter is varied while all others remain at their standard values as detailed in Table~\ref{Table1_Parameters .}
elements.sent-2411-350: A red vertical bar marks the standard value of the parameter being examined.
elements.sent-2411-351: Overall, dsODEs (the bottom map in each panel) quantitatively predict the locations of bifurcation points and the number of branches for spiking networks (the top map in each panel), accurately reflecting the correct beat numbers.
elements.sent-2411-352: However, due to noise, the LIF network dynamics may deviate from the attractors predicted by dsODEs, leading to less regular bifurcation maps or even additional peaks in the $\Delta \bar{m}$ distribution.
elements.sent-2411-353: Examples include $S^{EI < 2.65$ in Fig.~\ref{Fig7_bifurcations A, $S^{II > 2.45$ in Fig.~\ref{Fig7_bifurcations C, and $p^{II \in (0.50, 0.62)$.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-354: Additionally, branches that are close together in the dsODE predictions may also be blurred by noise in the LIF dynamics, such as $p^{EI \in (0.5, 0.55)$ in Fig.~\ref{Fig7_bifurcations B and $\tau_E \in (2.5, 3.1)$.}}
elements.sent-2411-355: Another interesting finding is the chaos-like behavior of dsODEs when varying $\tau^{EE}$, the synaptic timescale between E neurons, while keeping the synaptic timescale for E to I projections fixed ($\tau^{EE \in (1.75, 2.40)$).}
elements.sent-2411-356: Despite the discrepancies above, we stress that the bifurcation maps predicted by the dsSDE scheme are strikingly similar to LIF networks (Fig.~\ref{FigS1_bifurcations_of_dsODE_noise ).}
elements.sent-2411-357: We find that adding noise terms back to the dsODE system successfully recovers the extra branches induced by the noise, as well as the blurring effect.

elements.sent-2411-358: Neural networks with finite numbers of neurons can exhibit intrinsic fluctuations and variability \cite{schwalger2017towards .}
elements.sent-2411-359: These finite-neuron effects are critical for accurately capturing the dynamics of real neural systems (e.g., minicolumns in the cerebral cortex consisting of only $O(10-10^3)$ neurons).
elements.sent-2411-360: Traditional mean-field approaches often assume $N \to \infty$ and weak coupling, neglecting the stochastic fluctuations present in finite systems \cite{brunel2000dynamics,cai2006kinetic,MontbrioEtAl2015 .}
elements.sent-2411-361: In previous studies, finite-neuron effects were typically explored through perturbations of mean-field theory \cite{el2009master,bressloff2010stochastic,trousdale2012impact,buice2010systematic,buice2013dynamic .}
elements.sent-2411-362: However, this approach often results in oversimplification.
elements.sent-2411-363: First, finite-neuron effects may induce synchronization properties and oscillatory behavior that are absent in the corresponding mean-field equations.
elements.sent-2411-364: Second, finite-size fluctuations become more pronounced around critical points in parameter space, potentially leading to transitions between different types of dynamics, as shown in Sect.~\ref{Sect4.3-1E1I and \ref{Sect4.4-2E1I .}}
elements.sent-2411-365: Moreover, case studies in \cite{joglekar2019case also demonstrated that scaling down a large SNN without parameter adjustment can lead to catastrophic results.}
elements.sent-2411-366: The finite-neuron effect arises from the discrete nature of neurons and spiking events.
elements.sent-2411-367: The impact of spikes is “quantum” rather than continuous.
elements.sent-2411-368: Therefore one spike alone may significantly alter the network dynamics, and the variability of individual neurons is not negligible.
elements.sent-2411-369: Generally, any mathematical theory of SNNs (including dsODE) based on differential equations must violate this discrete nature to some extent.
elements.sent-2411-370: This violation can sometimes be mitigated by adding noise terms back to the equations, such as the $\sqrt{\frac{f(t) {N }$ white noise used in previous studies, where $f(t)$ is the average firing rate of single neurons.}}
elements.sent-2411-371: However, the effectiveness of this modification depends on two conditions: 1) whether the mathematical theory accurately captures the nonlinear network dynamics and the non-white stochasticity in $f(t)$ itself, and 2) whether the number of neurons is “small but large enough” for the Gaussian approximation to hold for the randomness in spike production.
elements.sent-2411-372: In dsODE theory, the neuron number N plays a role in two key aspects.
elements.sent-2411-373: First, when $f^R(t)$ is constant, Eq.~\ref{Sect3.1-Eq10-H gives $D^{QR /\mu^{QR = \frac{1 {2 + h^{QR (1 - p^{QR )$.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-374: To maintain the network firing rate as $N \to \infty$, we set $h^{QR \sim O(\frac{1 {N^R )$ while fixing $p^{QR}$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-375: As a result, $\mu^{QR}$ converges to a non-zero constant, and there is an $O(\frac{1 {N^R )$ deviation in the variance of recurrent input from the standard Ornstein-Uhlenbeck process.}}
elements.sent-2411-376: Second, the noise terms in Eq.~\ref{Sect3.2-Eq19-frNoise_Redu and \ref{Sect3.2-Eq19-vfluxNoise-Redu are related to \(N^Q_{\mbm^{th }\), the number of neurons in bin \(\mbm^{th}\), which is proportional to $N^Q$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-377: When $N^Q$ is very small, the Gaussian approximations for these noise terms break down.
elements.sent-2411-378: To assess how well dsODE captures the finite-neuron effect, we conducted a case study using parameter settings that produce the 2-beat dynamics in Fig.~\ref{Fig2_LIF_dynamics C ($S^{EI =$ 2.95 and $N =$ 400).}}
elements.sent-2411-379: When scaling the network size, we kept $S^{QR _N = S^{QR}\cdot 400/N$.}
elements.sent-2411-380: We found that the pure ODE system predictions for dynamics, firing rates, and synchrony (quantified as the spike synchrony index, see definition in \cite{rangan2013emergent ) were satisfactory for $N > 400$ (blue curves in Fig.~\ref{Fig8_finiteN ).}}
elements.sent-2411-381: Notably, the average firing rate per neuron was not constant for different $N$, making these results non-trivial.
elements.sent-2411-382: However, for very small N (e.g., $N = 40$ or 60), the amplitude of oscillatory dynamics and firing rates predicted by the ODE system were significantly different from the corresponding SNNs.
elements.sent-2411-383: Nevertheless, these discrepancies were partially mitigated by the dsSDE model, i.e., adding the noise terms from Eq.~\ref{Sect3.2-Eq19-frNoise_Redu and \ref{Sect3.2-Eq19-vfluxNoise-Redu back to dsODEs (green dashed curves in Fig.~\ref{Fig8_finiteN ).}}}
elements.sent-2411-384: A comprehensive investigation of the dsODE system’s performance with respect to finite-neuron effects is beyond the scope of this paper, though our case study shows promising results and can be potentially extended to other parameter regimes.
elements.sent-2411-385: Nonetheless, our results suggest that the CG-Markov model is a strong candidate for retaining finite-neuron effects, as it does not violate the discrete nature of neurons and spikes, warranting further in-depth analysis.
elements.section-2411-006: Comparison between dsODEs and previous population methods
elements.sent-2411-386: In this section, we discuss the relationships and differences between dsODEs and several classical approaches.
elements.sent-2411-387: We begin by comparing dsODEs with rate models (e.g., the Wilson-Cowan equations, \cite{wilson1973mathematical ) and Fokker-Planck (FP) equations \cite{brunel2000dynamics,cai2006kinetic,vinci2023self , two of the most widely used population-level methods for modeling spiking neural networks.}}
elements.sent-2411-388: We will also examine the dsODE framework in contrast with the refractory density method (RDM, \cite{schwalger2017towards ), a recent breakthrough in the field.}
elements.sent-2411-389: Similar to dsODEs, RDM does not rely on the assumptions of an infinite number of neurons or weak coupling weights, making it the first effective mathematical theory for modeling local neural circuits under realistic conditions.
elements.sent-2411-390: We further compare the predictive power of dsODEs, RDM, and a recent variant of FP equations that incorporates finite-neuron effects \cite{vinci2023self .}
elements.sent-2411-391: For simplicity, we focus on the long-term average firing rate \begin{equation}\label{Sect5-Eqn20-FRate} \bar{f} = \frac1T\lim_{T\to\infty}\int_{0}^{T}f(t)\,dt.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-392: Since the LIF equations in \cite{schwalger2017towards,vinci2023self are formulated slightly differently, we choose to adapt our dsODEs.}
elements.sent-2411-393: See simulation details of LIF networks, dsODEs, RDM, and FP equations in Appendix.
elements.sent-2411-394: Starting from the seminal work by Wilson and Cowan \cite{wilson1972excitatory,wilson1973mathematical , rate models have become perhaps the most widely used population method among brain and neuronal network modelers.}
elements.sent-2411-395: Rate models are typically employed to describe the average firing rates of neuronal populations without accounting for the precise timing of individual spikes.
elements.sent-2411-396: For example, a typical rate model for a network with a pair of E/I populations can be formulated as: \begin{subequations \begin{eqnarray}\tau^E\ddt{f^E} = -f^E + F^E(w^{EE}f^E + w^{EI}f^I+ I^E) \, , \tau^I\ddt{f^I} = -f^I + F^I(w^{IE}f^E + w^{II}f^I + I^I)\, ,\end{eqnarray} \end{subequations where $f^{E,I (t)$ denote the firing rates of E/I populations, $I^{E,I (t)$ represent external inputs to both populations, and $w^{QR}$ are the coupling strengths between populations.}}}}
elements.formula-2411-030: \begin{equation}\tau^E\ddt{f^E} = -f^E + F^E(w^{EE}f^E + w^{EI}f^I+ I^E) \, , \tau^I\ddt{f^I} = -f^I + F^I(w^{IE}f^E + w^{II}f^I + I^I)\, ,\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-397: Due to their lack of consideration for the detailed physiological processes of individual neurons, the interaction kernels for firing rates, $F^{E,I}$, are often assumed for analytical convenience or chosen to reflect specific dynamical regimes (e.g., rectified linear, sigmoid functions, and so on).
elements.sent-2411-398: Moreover, the reaction timescales, $\tau^{E,I}$, are typically chosen arbitrarily, even though in an SNN they should depend on many intrinsic features of the E/I populations (e.g., network architecture, neuronal adaptation, etc.) and may vary significantly under different dynamic regimes.
elements.sent-2411-399: Overall, rate models provide a macroscopic view of neural population activities.
elements.sent-2411-400: They are efficient for large-scale simulations and can offer rich analytical insights, but they are not designed to capture the fine temporal dynamics in SNNs and physiological details of single neurons.
elements.sent-2411-401: To illustrate this limitation, we perform a 1D parameter scan of $w^{EI}$, which is analogous to the I-to-E synaptic coupling weight $S^{EI}$ in Table~\ref{Table1_Parameters .}
elements.sent-2411-402: As shown in Fig.~\ref{FigS2_Rate_Model , the dynamics of Eq.~\ref{Sect5.1-Eqn22-RateModel undergo two Hopf bifurcations as $w^{EI}$ varies, transitioning first from a stable fixed point to a one-beat oscillation, and then returning to a stable fixed point.}}
elements.sent-2411-403: In contrast to the example shown in Fig.~\ref{Fig2_LIF_dynamics C, the rate model does not exhibit more complex dynamical structures, such as 2-beat oscillations or transitions between homogeneous and synchronous states.}
elements.sent-2411-404: This limitation arises from the low dimensionality of the rate model: the “2-beat” dynamics are represented by trajectories with two overlapping cycles (Fig.~\ref{Fig5_1E1I_SEI CD), which require at least three dimensions in an ODE system.}
elements.sent-2411-405: Due to its formulation, Eq.~\ref{Sect5.1-Eqn22-RateModel is inherently restricted to two dependent variables, limiting its ability to represent higher-dimensional dynamics.}
elements.sent-2411-406: Using principles from statistical physics, the Fokker-Planck (FP) equation describes the temporal evolution of the probability density function for the states of neurons, typically under the assumption $N \to \infty$.
elements.sent-2411-407: For example, the FP equation for a single E-population of LIF neurons can be formulated as follows (by removing the inhibitory terms from Eq.~\ref{Sect2.1-Eqn1-LIF ): \begin{equation}\nonumber \partial_t \rho(v,g^E,t) =\, \partial_v\left\{\left[g^{E\rm{leak}}v + g^E\left(v-\varepsilon^{E}\right)\right]\rho\right\} + \lambda^E\left[\rho\left(v-S^{E\rm{ext}},g^E,t\right) - \rho(v,g^E,t)\right] \label{Sect5-eq21-FP} + \partial_{g^E}\left(\frac{g^E}{\tau^E}\rho\right) + pf^E(t)N^E\left[\rho\left(v,g^E-\frac{S^{EE}}{\tau^E},t\right) - \rho(v,g^E,t)\right],\end{equation} where the firing rate is determined by the probability flux crossing $V^{th}$: \begin{equation}f^E(t) = \int_0^\infty dg^E\cdot\left\{-\left[g^{E\rm{leak}}v + g^E\left(v-\varepsilon^{E}\right)\right]\rho + \lambda^E\int_{V^{th}-S^{E\rm{ext}}}^{V^{th}}dv\cdot\rho\left(v-S^{E\rm{ext}},g^E,t\right)\right\}.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-408: FP equations and their variants (such as kinetic theories) have been highly successful in making quantitative predictions for various regimes of neural dynamics.
elements.sent-2411-409: However, these methods are constrained by the assumption $N \to \infty$, which is necessary to justify the probability density function $\rho(v,g^E,t)$.
elements.sent-2411-410: This leads to two main limitations.
elements.sent-2411-411: First, Eq.~\ref{Sect5-eq21-FP requires $S^{EE \to 0$, so that the impact of individual spikes becomes negligible (a condition often referred to as the “weak-coupling” assumption in many studies), thereby enabling the derivation of a differential equation.}}
elements.sent-2411-412: Second, Eq.~\ref{Sect5-eq21-FP requires a low degree of synchrony, so that the recurrent input can be approximated as a Poisson process.}
elements.sent-2411-413: Otherwise, the equation would blow up when a significant fraction of the population fires within a short time window, such as in the spiking clusters observed in Fig.~\ref{Fig2_LIF_dynamics .}
elements.sent-2411-414: In fact, analytical work has shown that Eq.~\ref{Sect5-eq21-FP may blow up when $\tau^E$ is small \cite{caceres2014beyond .}}
elements.sent-2411-415: Furthermore, implementing the fire-and-reset physiology necessitates complex boundary conditions.
elements.sent-2411-416: When $\tau^{\rm{ref = 0$, the probability flux crossing $V^{th}$ must be directly added back to the rest potential $\varepsilon^{\mathrm{rest = 0$, and for non-zero refractory periods, this can introduce additional analytical challenges.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-417: The dsODE system shares several conceptual similarities with the FP method and its variants but differs in two key aspects.
elements.sent-2411-418: First, dsODE does not require $N \to \infty$ and, consequently, is not limited to the weak coupling regime.
elements.sent-2411-419: Instead, it directly counts the number of neurons in each state, thereby accounting for finite-neuron effects.
elements.sent-2411-420: Second, unlike the blowup behavior of FP equations, dsODE successfully captures different levels of network synchrony (see Sect.~\ref{Sect4.3-1E1I ).}
elements.sent-2411-421: This robustness is attributed to dsODEs being based on discrete neuron counts rather than continuous density functions.
elements.sent-2411-422: The difference arises in their derivations: FP methods require $\delta v \to 0$ at first to obtain the probability distribution over a continuous domain of $v$, thus needing to assume low levels of correlation between recurrent inputs in a finite $\delta t$.
elements.sent-2411-423: In contrast, dsODEs first take $\delta t \to 0$, while sacrificing the “spatial resolution” of the membrane potential distribution by introducing discrete $v$-states.
elements.sent-2411-424: This trade-off relieves dsODEs from the low-synchrony assumption, enabling them to handle varying levels of transient synchrony.
elements.sent-2411-425: Additionally, the use of discrete $v$-states accommodates the fire-and-reset physiology (even when $\tau^{\rm{ref > 0$) without introducing cumbersome boundary conditions.}}
elements.sent-2411-426: On the other hand, the form of dsODEs converges to FP equations when $N \to \infty$ and the bin size $a \to 0$ (while ignoring all noise terms).
elements.sent-2411-427: However, whether the dynamics of dsODEs converge back to those of FP equations is beyond the scope of this study.
elements.sent-2411-428: Nevertheless, we point out that the core assumption of dsODEs is to decorrelate $v$ and $g^E$.
elements.sent-2411-429: Conversely, the kinetic equation (Eq.~4.19 in \cite{cai2006kinetic ) chooses not to decorrelate $v$ and $g^E$, instead employing dimensional reduction techniques such as moment closures.}
elements.sent-2411-430: This allows the kinetic theory to better capture low-firing rate regimes (e.g., background activity) where our Assumption 1 may not hold.
elements.sent-2411-431: Finally, we compare the predictive power of dsODEs to a recent FP-based method by Vinci et al. \cite{vinci2023self .}
elements.sent-2411-432: In this study, Vinci et al. derived a self-consistent stochastic partial differential equation for an FP model of single finite-size E-populations.
elements.sent-2411-433: They first derived the power spectrum of finite-size noise in the firing rate for an uncoupled population and then extended it to coupled populations using a Markov embedding technique.
elements.sent-2411-434: Their results worked well for the parameter regimes they investigated when $N > 1000$.
elements.sent-2411-435: However, we note that Vinci et al. restricted their study to relatively weak coupling regimes (despite their claim to have tested strong coupling).
elements.sent-2411-436: In their setup, the total contribution of a spike to the membrane potentials of all postsynaptic neurons, $KJ$, is restricted to $<0.6(V^{th -V^{\rm rest )$, where $K$ is the number of postsynaptic contacts per neuron and $J$ is the synaptic coupling weight.}}
elements.sent-2411-437: This value is significantly lower than the approximately $10(V^{th -V^{\rm rest )$ observed in the macaque V1 model \cite{chariker2016orientation , and the tested range in our study is approximately $[4.5, 54]\cdot(V^{th -V^{\rm rest )$.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-438: We tested both dsODE and Vinci et al.’s method on the long-term firing rates of single E-populations with $10^3$ and $10^4$ neurons on our parameter sets with stronger couplings between neurons.
elements.sent-2411-439: We choose to vary four parameters, one representing a kind: $\lambda^E$ (rate of external stimuli), $p^{EE}$ (projection probability), $S^{EE}$ (synaptic coupling weight), and $\tau^E$ (synaptic timescale).
elements.sent-2411-440: The ranges of tested parameters are shown in Fig.~\ref{Fig9_Comparison_with_previous_methods A, with other parameters listed in Table~\ref{Table1_Parameters .}}
elements.sent-2411-441: We found that Vinci et al.’s FP method can yield a predictive error exceeding 10\% for firing rates, whereas dsODEs achieve an error less than 5\%.
elements.sent-2411-442: The Refractory Density Method (RDM) and the dsODE system share a number of conceptual similarities, as both are designed to model homogeneous networks and utilize coarse-graining techniques to simplify the underlying dynamics of spiking neural networks (SNNs).
elements.sent-2411-443: Additionally, both methods discretize neuron states and deal directly with the number of neurons in each state, rather than relying on a continuous density function.
elements.sent-2411-444: This shared feature enables both methods to capture finite-neuron fluctuations, a crucial aspect of accurately representing the dynamics of real neural circuits with relatively small neuron populations.
elements.sent-2411-445: The key distinction between the two methods lies in how they discretize neuron states.
elements.sent-2411-446: RDM discretizes neuron states based on the timing since the last spike of each neuron, whereas dsODEs discretize neuron states based on physiological variables (for LIF neurons, the membrane potentials).
elements.sent-2411-447: This difference has important implications for the applicability and performance of each method.
elements.sent-2411-448: By focusing on the timing of the last spike, RDM inherently assumes that all neurons within the population receive identical inputs—both external and recurrent.
elements.sent-2411-449: Consequently, any noise or variability in synaptic currents is aggregated and reflected as a “soft threshold,” wherein randomness is introduced in the firing threshold of neurons.
elements.sent-2411-450: While this approach simplifies the analysis and implementation of the method, it introduces limitations when the synaptic noise or recurrent input variabilities are highly correlated across the population.
elements.sent-2411-451: We validate this statement by directly comparing the numerical simulations of SNNs (one E \& one I-populations, the same as Sect.~\ref{Sect4.3-1E1I ), dsODEs, and RDM with the same choices of parameters.}
elements.sent-2411-452: We find that the limitations in RDM become particularly evident in regimes with different levels of synchrony among neurons.
elements.sent-2411-453: When the network exhibits high synchrony (e.g., during $\tau^E<2$), the assumption of independent synaptic inputs breaks down, causing RDM to inaccurately capture the dynamics.
elements.sent-2411-454: This results in prediction errors larger than those of dsODEs, which directly model the interactions between discrete voltage states and account for correlated fluctuations in synaptic currents.
elements.sent-2411-455: On the other hand, both dsODE and RDM converge to similar results under conditions of low synchrony (e.g., during $\tau^E>2$).
elements.sent-2411-456: We have also compared dsODEs and RDM by varying the expectation of external stimuli and projection probability between neurons.
elements.sent-2411-457: We find that, for different external stimuli, the prediction errors of dsODE are in general one order magnitude smaller than RDM, regardless of the size of SNNs.
elements.sent-2411-458: A similar trend is observed for different $p$.
elements.sent-2411-459: In all, our results suggest that while both methods are effective in capturing macroscopic behaviors, dsODEs provide a more nuanced understanding of network dynamics in challenging scenarios such as high synchrony or correlated input variability.

elements.sent-2411-460: This study presented a novel discretized-state ordinary differential equation (dsODE) framework, offering a robust method to model the intricate dynamics of spiking neural networks (SNNs) with minimal information loss.
elements.sent-2411-461: By integrating principles of Markov models and coarse-graining, our dsODE system bridged the gap between microscopic neuronal activities and mesoscopic network behaviors, providing a powerful tool for studying emergent dynamics in local neural circuits.
elements.sent-2411-462: Our framework not only captured the physiological properties of individual neurons but also illuminated their impact on network-level dynamics, positioning dsODE as a valuable addition to the computational modeling of dynamical systems.
elements.sent-2411-463: In the rest of this section, we first consider the details underlying the effectiveness of the dsODE framework.
elements.sent-2411-464: Second, we discuss some technical issues and limitations of the dsODE.
elements.sent-2411-465: Then, we highlight the mathematical advantages of the dsODE, before we conclude by discussing a few promising future directions.. \heading{Effectiveness.}
elements.sent-2411-466: The dsODE framework builds upon the Markovian modeling methods of spiking networks, which were first introduced by Li et al. \cite{li2019well and later employed to study SNN dynamics in a series of works \cite{li2019stochastic, cai2021model, zhang2024learning .}}
elements.sent-2411-467: These earlier Markov models were not designed to mimic the physiological details of single neurons and the interactions among them.
elements.sent-2411-468: In contrast, the dsODE system explicitly integrates the leaky integrate-and-fire descriptions, providing a robust mathematical tool for approximating spiking network behavior while maintaining fidelity to the underlying neural processes.
elements.sent-2411-469: A key innovation of the dsODE system lies in its derivation, which employs three critical approximations: (1) discretization of voltage states to reduce the complexity of the system, (2) a fast self-decorrelation assumption to derive a coarse-grained (CG) Markov model that captures the transition dynamics between voltage states, and (3) a Gaussian approximation for random variables to derive a set of ODEs.
elements.sent-2411-470: These approximations ensure that the framework remains analytically tractable while preserving the essential features of spiking neural network dynamics.
elements.sent-2411-471: Our results show that dsODE maintains a balance between computational efficiency, analyzability, and biological realism.
elements.sent-2411-472: It makes the dsODE system highly appealing for modeling local neural circuits such as minicolumns and hypercolumns in cortex.
elements.sent-2411-473: The methodological robustness of the dsODE framework is particularly significant when contrasted with traditional machine learning (ML) models.
elements.sent-2411-474: While ML models are often effective at interpolation within the range of training data, they are typically not designed to extrapolate to unseen parameter regimes or to detect bifurcations—dynamical transitions in the system’s phase space \cite{rudy2017data,lusch2018deep,recht2019tour .}
elements.sent-2411-475: Nevertheless, recent studies reveal that embedding information of related physics or system structure can substantially enhance the ability of ML models to extrapolate and detect bifurcations in differential equations \cite{raissi2019physics,brunton2016discovering,schaeffer2017learning,beregi2023using .}
elements.sent-2411-476: The dsODE system, on the other hand, offers a principled approach for predicting the evolution of SNNs as network parameters are varied.
elements.sent-2411-477: This predictive capability makes dsODEs a promising candidate for integration with ML methods in hybrid modeling approaches.
elements.sent-2411-478: Such a combination could enhance our understanding of how spiking networks transition between different dynamical regimes, potentially providing insights into how the brain integrates diverse functions within the same neural circuits.. \heading{Limitations.}
elements.sent-2411-479: While the dsODE system presents a significant advancement in mathematical theories of spiking networks, certain limitations warrant further investigation.
elements.sent-2411-480: First, the discretization of voltage states, though computationally efficient, may introduce artifacts that deviate from the continuous dynamics of leaky integrate-and-fire (LIF) neurons.
elements.sent-2411-481: This challenge becomes more pronounced when applying the dsODE system to more complex neuron models, such as FitzHugh-Nagumo or Hodgkin-Huxley, where the underlying dynamics are more intricate \cite{hodgkin1952quantitative,fitzhugh1961impulses .}
elements.sent-2411-482: In such cases, more refined discretization techniques, such as adaptive state partitioning (see, e.g., \cite{munsky2006finite,deuflhard2008adaptive ), may be necessary to accurately model local circuit phenomena, especially those with neurons with adaptation or bursting behaviors.}
elements.sent-2411-483: Second, the fast self-decorrelation assumption (the key simplification that enabled the derivation of the coarse-grained Markov model) may break down in certain scenarios—particularly in networks with highly biased architectures, long synaptic time constants, or regimes with extremely low firing rates.
elements.sent-2411-484: Addressing this limitation will likely require extending the dsODE system to incorporate non-Markovian dynamics, which would likely better capture long-range correlations and temporal dependencies.
elements.sent-2411-485: Furthermore, extending the model to heterogeneous networks—where neurons exhibit varied biophysical properties or diverse connectivity patterns—would enable a more comprehensive analysis of how network heterogeneity influences overall dynamics and stability.. \heading{Mathematical advantages.}
elements.sent-2411-486: Despite these challenges, the Markovian framework introduced in this study offers substantial analytical benefits.
elements.sent-2411-487: For example, the invariant measure of a Markov chain, which can be computed from its transition matrix, provides complete information about the system’s asymptotic behavior.
elements.sent-2411-488: This approach has been successfully applied previously \cite{cai2021model , where the invariant measure was used to capture distinct types of Gamma oscillations in simple spiking networks.}
elements.sent-2411-489: Moreover, based on established proofs of ergodicity \cite{li2019well , a large deviation analysis could be applied to Markovian spiking networks to compute transition waiting times between multiple attractors.}
elements.sent-2411-490: This could offer valuable insights into decision-making processes and planning behaviors in animals by linking cognitive functions to underlying spiking network dynamics.
elements.sent-2411-491: Additionally, the Markovian framework simplifies the computation of adjoint flows \cite{plessix2006review , as it requires only the transposition of the probability transition matrix.}
elements.sent-2411-492: This feature enhances the efficiency of parameter exploration in spiking networks, typically a challenging task for biologically detailed models with many parameters.
elements.sent-2411-493: The dsODE framework opens new avenues for studying complex systems, particularly in neuroscience.
elements.sent-2411-494: Its capacity to model intricate network dynamics, such as partial synchrony in fluctuation-driven, low-rate regimes, makes it an ideal tool for investigating phenomena where synchrony and timing are critical.
elements.sent-2411-495: One promising direction is the incorporation of synaptic plasticity rules, which would enable exploration of learning and adaptation in SNNs from a dynamical systems perspective.
elements.sent-2411-496: Moreover, dsODEs could serve as building blocks for multi-scale cortical models, efficiently capturing interactions within and across multiple functional regions of the brain.

elements.section-2411-008: A piecewise linear scheme of voltage configurations
elements.sent-2411-499: In the dsODE framework, we represent the detailed voltage configuration $n^Q(v)$ as a piecewise linear distribution formed by connecting a sequence of turning points.
elements.sent-2411-500: Here, we provide a detailed description of the algorithm based on this configuration.
elements.sent-2411-501: For each combined voltage bin $I_\mbm =[\mbm\ba,(\mbm+1)\ba)$, we select a turning point $(x^Q_\mbm, y^Q_\mbm)$, where $x^Q_\mbm\in I_\mbm$ and $y^Q_\mbm \geq 0$, and then connect each pair of adjacent turning points linearly.
elements.sent-2411-502: Consequently, the voltage configuration is expressed as: \begin{equation}n^Q(v) = k^Q_{\mbm}v + b^Q_{\mbm}, \quad x^Q_{\mbm} < v < x^Q_{\mbm+1}\end{equation} where $\mbm \in \{-\mbm^{I , -\mbm^{I + 1, … , \mbm^{th}\}$ represents the voltage bin partitions.}}
elements.sent-2411-503: Here, $k^Q_{\mbm =(y^Q_{\mbm+1 -y^Q_{\mbm )/(x^Q_{\mbm+1 -x^Q_{\mbm )$ is the slope, and $b^Q_{\mbm = y^Q_{\mbm - k^Q_{\mbm x^Q_{\mbm}$ is the intercept.}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-504: To close the piecewise linear representation at both ends, we set $(x^Q_{-\mbm^{I -1 , y^Q_{-\mbm^{I -1 ) = (-\varepsilon^{I ,0)$ and $(x^Q_{m^{th +1 , y^Q_{m^{th +1 ) = (V^{th ,0)$.}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-505: Thus, the entire voltage configuration is defined by $2\mathbf{M}$ parameters.
elements.sent-2411-507: Finally, the numerical solution of dsODE for one timestep should first evolve $N^Q_\mbm$ and mean voltage $\bar{v ^Q_\mbm$, then update the turning points $(x^Q_{\mbm (t), y^Q_{\mbm (t))$ summarized following the steps below:.}}}
elements.sent-2411-509: Evolve $N_{\mbm ^{Q}$ and $\bar{v _{\mbm ^{Q}$ for one timestep $\delta t$ with the Euler forward scheme, using Eqs.~\ref{Sect3.2-Eq16-J_Redu through~\ref{Sect3.2-Eq18-vflux-Redu .}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-510: Infer $\left\{x^Q_{\mbm (t+dt), y^Q_{\mbm (t+dt)\right\} = \mathbf{F ^{-1}\left(\left\{N_{\mbm ^{Q (t+dt), \bar{v _{\mbm ^{Q (t+dt)\right\}\right)$ via the Newton-Raphson method \cite{ypma1995historical .}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-511: This step involves solving for $2\mathbf{M}$ unknown variables from $2\mathbf{M}$ equations.
elements.sent-2411-512: On the other hand, $\mathbf{F ^{-1}$ is not guaranteed as well defined for arbitrary $\left\{N_{\mbm ^{Q (t+dt), \bar{v _{\mbm ^{Q (t+dt)\right\}$, and the algorithm will return failure in those cases.}}}}}}

elements.algorithm-2411-001: \caption{Evolution of dsODE for one timestep $\delta t$}\label{alg:one} \KwResult{Update turning points $\left\{x^Q_{\mbm},y^Q_{\mbm}\right\}$} Set network parameters $S^{QR}, p^{QR}, \tau^{R},\tau^{\rm ref}$, etc.; $\mathbf{M} \gets $ number of bins; $\delta t \gets$ timestep\; $\left\{x^Q_{\mbm}(t),y^Q_{\mbm}(t)\right\} \gets$ initial values of turning points\; $N^Q_\mathcal{R} \gets$ initial number of neurons in refractory state\; $\bm{u}^{QE},\bm{u}^{QI},\bm{D}^{QE},\bm{D}^{QI} \gets$ initial mean and variance of ($H^{QE}$,$H^{QI}$) \Comment*[r]{See definitions in Sect. 3.1.1} $(e, \ba, A_{\rm max},i_{\rm max})\gets $ termination tolerance, noise of initial guess, maximum attempts, maximum iterations in Newton-Raphson method\; \For{$\mathbf{m} \in\mathbf{\Gamma}\setminus\{\mathcal{R}\}$}{ \Comment{Compute $N_{\mathbf{m}}^{Q}$ and $\bar{v}_{\mathbf{m}}^{Q}$ for each voltage bin from turning points $\left\{x^Q_{\mathbf{m}}(t),y^Q_{\mathbf{m}}(t)\right\}$} $N^Q_\mbm \gets$ $r.h.s.$ of Eq.~\ref{SectA.1-Eq24-numofneuron_Redu}\; $\bar{v}^Q_\mbm\gets$ $r.h.s.$ of Eq.~\ref{SectA.1-Eq25-meanv_Redu}\; } \For{$\mathbf{m} \in\mathbf{\Gamma}$}{ Evolve $N_{\mathbf{m}}^{Q}$ and $\bar{v}_{\mathbf{m}}^{Q}$ for $\delta t$ following Eqs.~\ref{Sect3.2-Eq15-nDiscrete_Redu} and \ref{Sect3.2-Eq18-vflux-Redu} \; } \Comment{Update turning points with Newton-Raphson method} $A \gets 0$ \Comment*[r]{$A$ is the number of attempts that has been made} \While {$A=0$ \bf{or} isinvalid$(\left\{x^Q_{\mbm}(t+\delta t),y^Q_{\mbm}(t+\delta t)\right\})$}{ \Comment{isinvalid is a function to judge the validity of solution. See \bf{Algorithm 2}} \If{$A>A_{\rm max}$}{ \bf{return} failure\; } \eIf{$A=0$}{ $\vec{X}_0\gets\left\{x^Q_{\mbm}(t),y^Q_{\mbm}(t)\right\}$ }{ \Comment{Starting from the last guess and make another attempt} $\delta \vec{X}_0\gets \frac12\ba\cdot$rand$(2\mathbf{M})$ \Comment*[r]{uniform perturbations to the original guess} $\vec{X}_0\gets\left\{x^Q_{\mbm}(t),y^Q_{\mbm}(t)\right\} + \delta \vec{X}_0$ } norm$\gets e+1$ \Comment*[r]{set initial value of norm, which is the $\mathcal{L}^2$ norm of $\delta\vec{X}$} $i \gets 0$ \Comment*[r]{$i$ is the iteration number} \While {norm$>e$ \bf{and} $i<i_{\rm max}$}{ $\delta \vec{X}_{i+1}\gets \mathbf{J}_\mathbf{F}^{-1}(\vec{X}_{i})\cdot\delta\vec{X}_{i}$ \Comment*[r]{$\mathbf{J}_\mathbf{F}(\vec{X}_{i})$ is the Jacobian matrix of mapping $\mathbf{F}$ evaluated at $\vec{X}_{i}$} $\vec{X}_{i+1} \gets \vec{X}_{i}-\delta \vec{X}_{i+1}$\; norm $\gets ||\delta \vec{X}_{i+1}||_{\mathcal{L}^2}$\; $i \gets i+1$ } $\left\{x^Q_{\mbm}(t+\delta t),y^Q_{\mbm}(t+\delta t)\right\} \gets \vec{X}_A$\; $A\gets A+1$ } \bf{return} $\left\{x^Q_{\mbm}(t+\delta t),y^Q_{\mbm}(t+\delta t)\right\}$\;

elements.sent-2411-515: We here present the details for the expectation of recurrent E-fluxes, and the expectation for recurrent I-fluxes is similar.. \begin{equation}E[J^{QE}(v)] = \lim_{a\to0}\left\{-\int_v^{v+a\delta^E(v)} \frac{D^{QE}}{2(\tau^E)^2} n^Q(x) dx\, +\, \int_{v^*}^{v} \left[\frac{u^{QE}}{\tau^E} + \frac{D^{QE}}{2(\tau^E)^2}\right] n^Q(x) dx \right\} = \lim_{a\to0}\left\{\int_{v^*}^{v} \frac{u^{QE}}{\tau^E} n^Q(x) dx\,+\,\frac{D^{QE}}{2(\tau^E)^2}\left[\int_{v^*}^{v} \left(n^Q(x) - n^Q(x + a\delta v^*)\right) dx\,+\,\int_{v+a\delta^E{v}}^{v+a\delta^E{v^*}} n^Q(x)dx\right]\right\} = \frac{u^{QE}}{\tau^E}(\varepsilon^{E}-v)n^Q(v) +\lim_{a\to0}\left\{ \frac{D^{QE}}{2(\tau^{E})^2}\left[\frac{v-v^*}{a}\cdot\frac{n^Q(v)-n^Q(v^*)}{a} +\frac{a(\delta^E(v^*) - \delta^E(v))}{a^2}n^Q(v)\right]\right\} = \left(\frac{u^{QE}}{\tau^E} + \frac{D^{QE}}{2(\tau^{E})^2}\right)\cdot(\varepsilon^{E}-v)n^Q(v) - \frac{D^{QE}}{2(\tau^{E})^2}\cdot(\varepsilon^{E}-v)^2\frac{\partial n^Q}{\partial v} \,.\end{equation}.

elements.section-2411-010: A more efficient numerical implementation of dsODE
elements.sent-2411-516: There are infinitely many possible ways to infer the detailed voltage configuration $\{n^Q_m|[m/L]=\mbm\}$.
elements.sent-2411-517: However, our empirical findings suggest that, when the combined voltage bin size $\ba$ is sufficiently small, the neuron distribution within each voltage bin can be approximated as uniform.
elements.sent-2411-518: Although this approximation may affect the flux terms at interval boundaries in the dsODE model (e.g., producing infinitely large flux as timestep $\delta t \to 0$ while fixing $\ba$), dsODE remains accurate with the uniform scheme, provided that the timestep $\delta t$ is aligned with the combined voltage bin size $\ba$ (i.e., smaller $\ba$ necessitates a smaller $\delta t$).

elements.section-2411-012: Simulations of spiking networks, dsODE, Fokker-Planck, and refractory density methods
elements.sent-2411-536: The biological parameters listed in Tables 2 and 3 were used to simulate spiking networks, the dsODE method, and prior population-based methods.
elements.sent-2411-537: For the dsODE simulations, we employed the efficient numerical implementation outlined in Appendix A3, using a voltage interval length of 1 mV.
elements.sent-2411-538: To assess the predictive accuracy of each model, we calculated the relative firing rate errors by comparing each model’s output to that of its corresponding LIF network.
elements.sent-2411-539: The relative error was defined as: \begin{equation}Relative Error = \frac{|\bar{f}_{test} - \bar{f}_{LIF}|}{\bar{f}_{LIF}} \times 100\%\end{equation} where $f$'s are defined by Eq.~\ref{Sect5-Eqn20-FRate .}
elements.sent-2411-540: Simulations for each model used a timestep of $\delta t = 0.1$ ms and ran for a total of 10 seconds, with $\bar{f _{\text{test }$ calculated every 1 second to obtain the mean firing rate and standard error.}}
elements.sent-2411-541: It is worth noting that, in previous publications, refractory density methods (RDM) and Fokker-Planck (FP) equations utilized different physiological setups for spiking networks than those used in this study.
elements.sent-2411-542: The most significant difference is that \cite{schwalger2017towards implemented RDM on current-based integrate-fire networks, instead of conductance-based as in this study.}
elements.sent-2411-543: Also, \cite{schwalger2017towards and \cite{vinci2023self choose set the threshold potential $V^{th = 15$ mV.}}}
elements.sent-2411-544: For comparisons with RDM and FP, we adapted the dsODE model to match these setups accordingly.
elements.sent-2411-545: The source code for the Fokker-Planck and refractory density methods are available in \cite{vinci2023self and \cite{schwalger2017towards .}}
elements.sent-2411-546: Code for dsODE, as well as scripts for comparison with RDM and FP, can be accessed at https://github.com/changjie98/dsODE.
elements.sent-2411-547: Spike synchrony index (SSI) describes the degree of synchrony of the firing events as follows.
elements.sent-2411-548: In Fig.~\ref{Fig8_finiteN C, We borrow the definition of SSI from Chariker et al\cite{chariker2018rhythm and calculate the SSI values for networks of different scales.}}
elements.sent-2411-549: For each spike occurring at $t$, consider a $w$ ms time window centered by the spike $(t-w/2, t+w/2)$ and count the fraction of neurons in the whole network firing in such window.
elements.sent-2411-550: On the other hand, since dsODE does not produce any discrete spike, we use Eq.~\ref{Sect3.2-Eq21-vfluxNoise-Redu to compute the SSI of dsODE: \begin{equation}\label{Sect3.2-Eq21-vfluxNoise-Redu} SSI=\frac{\int_{w/2}^{t_{end}-w/2}\left(\int_{t-w/2}^{t+w/2}f^Q(\tau)\,d\tau \right)\cdot f^Q(t)\, dt}{N^Q\cdot \int_{w/2}^{t_{end}-w/2}f^Q(t)\,dt}\end{equation} It is not hard to see that SSI is larger for more synchronous spiking patterns.}
elements.sent-2411-551: For the completely synchronized dynamics, every other neuron fires within the time window of each spike hence SSI=1.
